comments.text: It seems like Simpson was not the first to prove that the density is minimized when all $a_i$ are equal. This is the first theorem in Section 5.3 of H. Halberstam and K. F. Roth, Sequences, Springer, New York, 1983, which was published 3 years prior to the Simpson paper referenced. They attribute the theorem to private communications with C. A. Rogers.
I recently wrote about Rogers theorem here, as it also leads to a quick proof of a separate Erdos problem.

I believe we can infer such difficulties by looking at, say, [345] and [434], where the reason they are (apparently) intractable is because there is no simple, polynomial formula for the functions considered, making delicate estimates needed to optimize them possibly out of question. The examples given are related to Frobenius problem and thresholds of completeness, known for not having explicit, polynomial formulae. This problem faces this exact obstacle, since it is asking for a formula where a simple and universal one might not exist.

I have put the following paper on Arxiv; [2508.18270]. It gives argument that the first question should not have a solution as hoped for. The title could have been "Trying to convince others it is impossible..." (would have been ugly title), as the arguments are not waterproof of course. E.g., the formula above for the minimum has also $2^n$ terms. So wondering about others opinions... How to conclude that some questions in this database are just fully impossible, and how do we consider them?
